sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=58, col=51).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 42, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 8 of about 72 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the blue blob had no impact (no_impact=True). This means clicking the blue blob is not the right action right now, or the level is not ready to be completed.
Let me think about what else I can do. In Level 1, I needed to set all buttons to OFF before clicking the blue blob. In Level 2, maybe I need to set the buttons to match a specific pattern before clicking the blue blob.
Looking at the left blue square's pattern:
- Yellow cells at cols 8-9 (first button column)
- White cells at cols 11 and 13 (between/within button columns)
This might indicate that only the first column of buttons should be ON. Currently, all 4 columns in row 33 are ON (black tops). So I need to turn OFF the buttons in columns 2, 3, and 4 of row 33.
But wait, let me reconsider. The left blue square has:
- Yellow cells: rows 57-59, cols 8-9
- White cells: row 58, cols 11 and 13
The yellow cells span 3 rows (57-59), which might correspond to the 3 rows of buttons (rows 33-38, 39-44, 45-50). The white cells are at row 58, which is the middle row.
So maybe the pattern is:
- Column 1: all 3 buttons ON (yellow at rows 57-59)
- Column 2: middle button OFF (white at row 58, col 11)
- Column 3: middle button OFF (white at row 58, col 13)
- Column 4: no indication (maybe all OFF?)
Actually, this is getting too complicated. Let me try a simpler approach: look at the right panel's gray square pattern and see if it gives any clues.
The gray square at the bottom right:
- Row 52: BBBBBBWWWWWWWW
- Row 53: BBBBBBWBBBBBBB
This shows white cells at row 52, cols 46-53 (8 white cells) and row 53, col 46 (1 white cell).
This might represent the target configuration for the right panel's buttons or object.
Actually, let me look at the right panel's object. The right panel has a yellow 2x2 square at rows 24-27, cols 42-45. Maybe I need to move this object to match the pattern in the gray square.
But the gray square's pattern is at rows 52-53, which is at the bottom of the screen, not in the right panel.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the Level 1 solution to understand the pattern.
In Level 1, the goal was to set all buttons to OFF and click the blue blob. The buttons were in the left panel, and clicking them toggled them between ON and OFF.
In Level 2, there are two panels with buttons. Maybe the goal is to:
1. Set the left panel's buttons to match the pattern in the left blue square
2. Set the right panel's buttons to match the pattern in the gray square
3. Move the objects to the correct positions
4. Click the blue blob
Let me try clicking on a button in the left panel to see if it toggles.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on a button in the left panel to see if it toggles
# The left panel's row 33 buttons have black (B) tops at cols 7-9, 12-14, 17-19, 22-24
# Let me click on the center of the first button (row 33, col 8)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 8}])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

# Check if the button changed
current_ascii = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nLeft panel row 33 after click: {current_ascii[33][5:25]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
